Kaṃsa imprisons Vasudeva and Devakī, sends Keśī to kill the brothers, and plots to have them killed by the elephant Kuvalayāpīḍa and wrestlers during a bow sacrifice. He orders Akrūra to bring Krishna and Balarāma to Mathurā.

story.title: The Killing of Ariṣṭa and Kaṃsa's Plot
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vraja and the lead-up to Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Ariṣṭa, Nārada informs Kaṃsa, leading to Kaṃsa's immediate actions.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic fight with the bull demon, Nārada's revelation, Kaṃsa's rage, and the setup for the Mathurā arena.

teachings.teaching: Krishna protects his devotees and destroys demons, demonstrating divine intervention.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner purification where the divine force (Krishna) eliminates negative tendencies (Ariṣṭa) that disturb the mind and body.

teachings.teaching: Kaṃsa's fear of death leads him to violent actions, illustrating how ignorance and attachment cause suffering.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees Kaṃsa as the ego that, upon hearing the truth (Nārada's revelation), becomes agitated and tries to suppress the divine (Krishna) through force, but ultimately fails.

teachings.teaching: Akrūra's response to Kaṃsa shows equanimity and acceptance of divine will, a key yogic principle.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the attitude of a practitioner who performs actions without attachment to results, trusting in the divine plan.

chapter_summary: Krishna kills the demon Keshī in the form of a horse, then later kills the demon Vyoma (son of Maya) who had kidnapped cowherd boys. The sage Nārada visits Krishna, praises him, and prophesies his future deeds.

story.title: The Killing of Keśī and Vyoma
story.arc: Krishna's childhood pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kaṃsa sends demons to kill Krishna; before the killing of Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: High: Krishna's playful yet effortless defeat of a giant horse-demon and a shape-shifting demon, with Nārada's dramatic prophecy.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the Supreme Lord, the inner controller of all beings, and the witness in the heart.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate reality (Krishna) is the inner self (ātman) that is realized through meditation and pranayama.

teachings.teaching: The Lord descends to protect the righteous and destroy the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the dynamic of Kriya-Yoga: the practitioner must destroy inner demons (negative tendencies) and cultivate sattvic qualities.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's actions are effortless and playful (līlā).
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this teaches that spiritual practice should be performed with ease and joy, not strain.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme object of surrender and devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes bhakti as a key component of Kriya-Yoga; surrender to the divine is essential.

chapter_summary: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, reflecting on his good fortune to see Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma. Upon arrival, he worships their footprints, meets them, and is warmly received by Nanda and the brothers.

story.title: Akrūra's Journey to Gokula
story.arc: Akrūra travels from Mathurā to Gokula, meditating on Kṛṣṇa's glories, meets Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, and is warmly received.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kaṃsa's atrocities and before the killing of Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's internal monologue of ecstasy and humility, contrasted with his official mission as Kaṃsa's messenger, creates dramatic irony.

teachings.teaching: Seeing the Lord is the highest good, even for a lowly person.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization that the divine is accessible through sincere devotion, transcending one's apparent limitations.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's feet are the refuge from the fear of death (Kāla).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes surrender to the divine as the means to overcome the cycle of birth and death.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial, yet He favors His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that divine grace flows naturally to those who are receptive through devotion.

teachings.teaching: The purpose of human life is to abandon pride, fear, and sorrow through seeing and hearing about the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the essence of sādhana: turning the mind inward through śravaṇa and darśana.

chapter_summary: Akrura arrives in Vrindavan to take Krishna and Balarama to Mathura for the festival. The gopis lament their impending separation from Krishna. Krishna and Balarama depart with Akrura, and the gopis, after a period of grief, return to their homes. On the way, they stop at the Yamuna, where Akrura bathes and has a vision of the serpent Ananta Shesha with Krishna and Balarama on his lap.

story.title: Akrura's Arrival and the Gopis' Lament
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan to Mathura
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs shortly after Akrura's arrival in Vrindavan, before the festival in Mathura.
story.comic_potential: High - the emotional farewell of the gopis and Akrura's underwater vision provide dramatic and visual scenes.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna illustrates the pinnacle of devotion (bhakti), where the devotee feels complete separation (viraha) from the divine.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this viraha is the highest form of yoga, where the soul's longing for union with the Divine becomes the driving force for spiritual practice. The gopis' lament shows that true devotion transcends ritual and is rooted in emotional surrender.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's assurance to the gopis that he will return demonstrates that the divine is never truly separate from the devotee; separation is a test of faith.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the apparent absence of the Divine is a call to deepen one's inner practice. The promise of return symbolizes the eventual realization of the indwelling Self.

teachings.teaching: Akrura's vision of Ananta Shesha with Krishna and Balarama reveals the cosmic form of the Lord, showing that the divine is both immanent and transcendent.
teachings.lens_note: This vision corresponds to the Kriya-Yoga experience of inner samadhi, where the practitioner perceives the unity of all existence. The serpent symbolizes the kundalini energy, and Krishna and Balarama represent the dual aspects of consciousness and energy.

chapter_summary: Akrura, having arrived at Vrindavan, sees Krishna and Balarama and is overwhelmed with devotion. He offers a prayer describing the Lord's divine form and attendants, including the sages Sanaka, Narada, and Prahlada, and the goddesses Sri, Pushti, Gira, Kanti, Kirti, Tushti, Ila, Urja, Vidya, Avidya, Shakti, and Maya. He then prostrates and praises the Lord with a choked voice.

story.title: Akrura's Vision and Prayer
story.arc: Krishna's Vrindavan pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Akrura arrives in Vrindavan, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrura's overwhelmed devotion and the detailed description of divine attendants could be visually striking.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is surrounded by pure devotees and divine energies, and is served by them.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the inner state of a yogi who perceives the divine presence and the various aspects of consciousness (deities) as attendants of the Self.

teachings.teaching: True devotion leads to overwhelming love and tears of joy, as seen in Akrura's reaction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that kriya-yoga culminates in bhakti, where the practitioner experiences spontaneous emotional purification and ecstasy.

chapter_summary: Akrūra offers a profound prayer to Kṛṣṇa, acknowledging Him as the supreme cause of all causes, the primordial Nārāyaṇa. He describes how all elements, senses, and deities emanate from Him, yet they cannot know His true nature. Akrūra explains various paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, jñāna, and bhakti—all ultimately leading to Kṛṣṇa. He lists many avatāras (Matsya, Hayagrīva, Kūrma, Varāha, Narasiṃha, Vāmana, Paraśurāma, Rāma, Vāsudeva, Saṅkarṣaṇa, Pradyumna, Aniruddha, Buddha, Kalki) and praises Kṛṣṇa's cosmic form. Akrūra laments his own ignorance and material entanglement, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's lotus feet.

story.title: Akrūra's Prayer to Kṛṣṇa
story.arc: Akrūra, having been sent by Kaṃsa to bring Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma to Mathurā, takes the opportunity to offer a heartfelt prayer acknowledging Kṛṣṇa's supremacy and seeking His grace.
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs after Akrūra meets Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma on the way to Mathurā, just before the wrestling match with Kaṃsa's wrestlers.
story.comic_potential: Low; this is a devotional hymn with no dramatic action.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa is the ultimate cause of all causes, from whom everything emanates and in whom everything rests.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the realization of the Supreme Self as the source of all prāṇa and consciousness, the goal of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The elements, senses, and deities cannot know Kṛṣṇa's true nature because they are material and He is transcendental.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga teaching that the mind and senses must be transcended through meditation to perceive the Self.

teachings.teaching: All paths of worship—Vedic sacrifices, yoga, jñāna, and bhakti—ultimately lead to Kṛṣṇa, just as all rivers flow into the ocean.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga synthesizes these paths: karma-yoga purifies, jñāna-yoga enlightens, and bhakti-yoga unites with the Divine.

teachings.teaching: The three guṇas (sattva, rajas, tamas) are products of Kṛṣṇa's prakṛti and bind all beings from Brahmā down to the immovable.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the guṇas are seen as energies that the yogi must rise above through prāṇāyāma and meditation.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa's cosmic form includes all elements, directions, and beings; the universe is His body.
teachings.lens_note: This viśvarūpa vision is a direct experience in deep meditation, where the yogi sees the entire cosmos as the body of the Lord.

teachings.teaching: The jīva is deluded by māyā, identifying with the body and possessions, and wanders in saṃsāra.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga breaks this identification by turning the mind inward to the Self.

teachings.teaching: Akrūra confesses his inability to control the senses and mind, seeking refuge at Kṛṣṇa's feet.
teachings.lens_note: This humility is essential in Kriya-Yoga: surrender to the Guru or Divine is the foundation for spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Akrūra witnesses Kṛṣṇa's divine form in the Yamunā, then they proceed to Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma enter the city, are welcomed by the citizens, kill a washerman, bless a weaver, and receive garlands from Sudāmā, who prays for devotion.

story.title: Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma Enter Mathurā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Mathurā
story.timeline_note: After leaving Vṛndāvana, before killing Kaṃsa.
story.comic_potential: The washerman's beheading and the weaver's fashion show have visual humor.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's form is not separate from the universe; all wonders are in Him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Recognizing the divine in all phenomena is the essence of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial to all beings, but especially favors His devotees.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Devotion (bhakti) is the highest practice; the Lord reciprocates according to one's surrender.

teachings.teaching: True wealth is devotion to the Lord, not material opulence.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind, leading to unwavering devotion.

chapter_summary: In this chapter, Dhritarashtra asks Sanjaya what his sons and the Pandavas did on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. Sanjaya describes Duryodhana's address to Drona, listing the warriors on both sides, and the blowing of conches signaling the start of the war.

story.title: The Gathering on the Battlefield
story.arc: Kurukshetra War
story.timeline_note: Just before the start of the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic listing of warriors and the blowing of conches could be depicted as a grand, tense scene.

teachings.teaching: The importance of recognizing the sacredness of the battlefield (dharmakshetra) and the duty of warriors.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, the battlefield symbolizes the inner field of dharma where one must fight against inner enemies (vices) with the help of the divine charioteer (consciousness).

teachings.teaching: The need for proper alignment and protection of the army (spiritual practices) under the guidance of a wise leader.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret the army as the collection of one's energies and faculties, which must be organized and protected by the wisdom (Bhishma) or strength (Bhima) depending on the context.

teachings.teaching: The sound of conches symbolizes the awakening of divine consciousness and the call to action.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the blowing of conches represents the vibration of pranava (Om) that awakens the kundalini and prepares the practitioner for inner battle.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama enter Mathura, encounter the hunchbacked woman Trivakra who offers them sandalwood paste, Krishna straightens her back, then they break the great bow in the arena, kill the guards, and later attend the wrestling festival arranged by Kamsa, who is terrified by omens.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama in Mathura
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura leading to the killing of Kamsa
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Putana and other demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama visit Mathura for the first time.
story.comic_potential: The straightening of Trivakra's back and the breaking of the bow are visually striking and humorous.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's touch straightens the crooked, symbolizing spiritual transformation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the straightening of the kundalini and the purification of the nadis through divine contact.

teachings.teaching: The breaking of the bow signifies the destruction of ego and material pride.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets the bow as the mind's tension; breaking it is the cessation of mental modifications through yoga.

teachings.teaching: Kamsa's omens and fear represent the anxiety of the materialistic mind facing death.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these omens are signs of the impending dissolution of the false ego when one approaches self-realization.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, having heard the wrestling arena's drum, go to see it. At the entrance, Krishna kills the elephant Kuvalayapida, then enters the arena with Balarama. The people praise them, recounting their divine feats. The wrestler Canura challenges them to a match, and Krishna accepts.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Enter the Wrestling Arena
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall
story.timeline_note: After the killing of various demons in Vrindavan, Krishna and Balarama go to Mathura for the wrestling festival.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic elephant fight, crowd reactions, and the challenge of wrestlers.

teachings.teaching: One should please the king by acting in accordance with his wishes, as the people's welfare depends on it.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of aligning individual actions with the greater order (dharma) to maintain harmony.

teachings.teaching: Even in competition, one should ensure fairness and avoid adharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that yoga is skill in action; Krishna's insistence on equal opponents shows the importance of righteous conduct even in conflict.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama engage in wrestling matches with Kamsa's wrestlers, killing Canura, Mustika, Kuta, Sala, and Tosala. Krishna then leaps onto Kamsa's dais, drags him down, and kills him. Balarama kills Kamsa's brothers. The women of Kamsa's court lament. Krishna and Balarama release their parents Devaki and Vasudeva from bondage.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama Kill Kamsa's Wrestlers and Kamsa
story.arc: Kamsa's downfall and liberation of Devaki and Vasudeva
story.timeline_note: This chapter occurs during the wrestling match at Kamsa's arena, after Krishna and Balarama have entered Mathura.
story.comic_potential: High: Dramatic wrestling bouts, Krishna's leap onto the dais, and the comedic lament of Kamsa's wives.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (lila) of the Lord demonstrates that dharma ultimately triumphs over adharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this victory symbolizes the inner conquest of negative tendencies (kleshas) through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: One should not associate with a place where adharma prevails; the wise avoid such assemblies.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the practitioner must avoid environments that disturb mental equanimity and hinder spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate protector and source of all beings; those who oppose Him find no peace.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga principle of surrendering the ego to the divine will, recognizing that resistance leads to suffering.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama return to Mathura, reunite with their parents Vasudeva and Devaki, and console them. They install Ugrasena as king, welcome back the Yadava clans who had fled due to Kamsa, and send Nanda and the gopas back to Vrindavan with gifts. Krishna and Balarama undergo their sacred thread ceremony with Garga as teacher, then study under Sandipani in Avanti, learning all scriptures and arts in 64 days. To repay their guru, they retrieve his son from the ocean, killing the demon Panchajana and recovering the boy from Yama's city. The guru blesses them, and they return to Mathura.

story.title: Krishna and Balarama's Return to Mathura and Education
story.arc: Krishna's early life and education
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The retrieval of the guru's son from the ocean and Yama's city has epic adventure potential.

teachings.teaching: One must serve parents and elders; failing to do so leads to suffering after death.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of selfless service (seva) as a foundational practice for purifying the mind and preparing for higher yoga.

teachings.teaching: A guru's instruction should be followed with devotion; the guru is like a deity.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights guru-bhakti as essential for transmission of subtle knowledge; the student's attitude determines the depth of learning.

teachings.teaching: True knowledge is grasped instantly by the worthy; the Lord as teacher reveals all sciences.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the Kriya-Yoga principle that when the mind is purified and focused, higher knowledge is received intuitively, not through laborious study.

teachings.teaching: Guru-dakshina (repayment to the teacher) should be performed selflessly, even at great risk.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma sees this as a metaphor for the disciple's duty to embody and propagate the teachings, completing the cycle of grace.

chapter_summary: Uddhava is sent by Krishna to Vrindavan to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis who are suffering from separation. He delivers Krishna's message, describes Krishna's divine nature, and assures them of his return. The chapter ends with the gopis' morning activities.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and Uddhava's consolation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, before his return to Vrindavan.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' morning activities and their curiosity about Uddhava's chariot.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and equal to all; he has no favorites or enemies.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of non-dual awareness where the practitioner sees the same consciousness in all beings.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's incarnations are for the protection of the righteous and for his own pleasure.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that divine play (lila) is a teaching tool; the Lord's actions demonstrate how to act without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the inner self of all beings, the ultimate cause and sustainer.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of realizing the Self as the witness, distinct from the body and mind.

teachings.teaching: By remembering the Lord at the time of death, even with an impure mind, one attains liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that constant remembrance of the divine (Ishvara-pranidhana) purifies the mind and leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits the gopis of Vrindavan as a messenger from Krishna. The gopis express their intense separation and love for Krishna, questioning his faithfulness. Uddhava delivers Krishna's message, which includes philosophical teachings on the nature of the self and the importance of constant remembrance. The gopis, though comforted, remain deeply attached to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Visit to the Gopis
story.arc: Krishna's departure from Vrindavan and the gopis' separation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava is sent to console the gopis.
story.comic_potential: The gopis' dramatic lamentations and Uddhava's attempts to console them have comedic potential, especially the gopis' sarcastic remarks about Krishna's messenger.

teachings.teaching: The gopis describe the fickleness of worldly relationships, comparing them to bees that leave flowers after taking nectar.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the need to transcend attachment to temporary objects and focus on the eternal self.

teachings.teaching: Krishna teaches that he is present in all elements and is the support of the senses, mind, and life forces.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes realizing the omnipresence of the divine through inner meditation, aligning with this teaching.

teachings.teaching: The self is pure consciousness, distinct from the three states of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep, which are products of maya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga focuses on transcending these states to realize the witness consciousness, as taught here.

teachings.teaching: Constant remembrance of Krishna leads to liberation and union with him.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga advocates continuous meditation on the divine form to achieve oneness.

teachings.teaching: The gopis express that even knowing detachment is the highest happiness, their hope for Krishna remains unshakable.
teachings.lens_note: This paradox of desire for the divine is central to bhakti; Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees it as the driving force for spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the gopis and Nanda's family after Krishna's departure. He stays for several months, singing Krishna's pastimes and alleviating their grief. The gopis' intense love for Krishna is praised, and Uddhava returns to Mathura, conveying their devotion to Krishna.

story.title: Uddhava's Mission to Vrindavan
story.arc: Krishna's separation and the gopis' devotion
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's departure to Mathura, Uddhava visits Vrindavan to console the residents.
story.comic_potential: Uddhava's attempt to console the gopis, who are deeply absorbed in Krishna's love, could be portrayed with emotional depth and humor in their interactions.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' love for Krishna is the highest form of devotion, surpassing even that of great yogis and sages.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this exemplifies the pinnacle of bhakti-yoga where the practitioner's entire being is absorbed in the divine, transcending all worldly attachments.

teachings.teaching: Even those who are seemingly unqualified (like the gopis) can attain the highest spiritual perfection through pure devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is accessible to all, regardless of external purity; sincere practice and surrender yield the same result.

teachings.teaching: The dust of the gopis' feet is supremely purifying, and their songs about Krishna sanctify the three worlds.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the power of association with advanced devotees, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga for accelerating spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: The residents of Vrindavan pray for their mind, speech, and body to be always engaged in Krishna's service.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga ideal of integrating all aspects of life into a continuous offering to the divine.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the courtesan's house, grants her a boon, then goes to Akrura's house. Akrura praises Krishna and is sent to Hastinapura to check on the Pandavas.

story.title: Krishna's Visit to Akrura and the Courtesan
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Kamsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's playful visit to the courtesan and Akrura's elaborate praise.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme cause and pervades all beings.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Recognizing the divine presence in all actions purifies karma.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is not bound by the gunas or karma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Through disciplined action without attachment, one transcends the gunas.

teachings.teaching: The Lord incarnates to protect dharma and relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Divine descent empowers practitioners to align with cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Saints purify by their mere sight, unlike material objects.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Association with realized beings accelerates spiritual progress.

chapter_summary: Akrūra visits Hastinapura, meets with Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Bhīṣma, Vidura, Kuntī, and others. Kuntī laments her sons' plight and prays to Kṛṣṇa. Akrūra advises Dhṛtarāṣṭra to treat the Pāṇḍavas equally, but Dhṛtarāṣṭra admits his attachment to his sons. Akrūra returns to Dvārakā and reports to Rāma and Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: Akrūra's Mission to Hastinapura
story.arc: Pre-Kurukshetra diplomacy
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Akrūra's diplomatic visit, Kuntī's emotional plea, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's stubbornness.

teachings.teaching: One is born alone, dies alone, and experiences the results of one's actions alone.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the solitary nature of spiritual practice; each individual must perform their own kriya and face their own karma.

teachings.teaching: Wealth acquired by adharma is taken away by others; one who accumulates it is abandoned by wealth, life, and family, and falls into darkness.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as a warning against attachment to material gains from unethical actions, which distract from the yogic path.

teachings.teaching: This world is like a dream, illusion, and mental construct; one should control the mind and remain equal and peaceful.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that through meditation one realizes the world as māyā and attains inner equanimity.

teachings.teaching: No one can change the will of Īśvara, who descends to relieve the earth's burden.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as acknowledging divine will; the yogi surrenders to the cosmic plan.

teachings.teaching: Salutations to Parameśvara, whose māyā creates the world and who enters it, yet remains incomprehensible.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this points to the realization of the Supreme Self as both immanent and transcendent.

chapter_summary: Jarāsandha, king of Magadha, attacks Mathurā with a vast army to avenge his son-in-law Kaṃsa's death. Kṛṣṇa and Balarāma, recognizing their divine purpose to relieve the earth's burden, engage in battle. Kṛṣṇa decimates the enemy forces, while Balarāma captures Jarāsandha but is restrained by Kṛṣṇa from killing him. Jarāsandha is released and later returns seventeen more times, each time defeated. After the eighteenth battle is imminent, the Yavana king Kālayavana, sent by Nārada, besieges Mathurā. Kṛṣṇa, to protect the Yadus, builds a fortress city in the sea, Dvārakā.

story.title: Jarāsandha's Attack and the Building of Dvārakā
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's early life and establishment of Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After Kaṃsa's death, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High: epic battles, divine weapons, strategic retreat, and city-building.

teachings.teaching: Kṛṣṇa incarnates to relieve the earth's burden and protect the righteous.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of dharma-rakṣā: the divine descends to restore balance when adharma prevails, akin to the yogi's inner battle to purify the mind.

teachings.teaching: True heroes do not boast but demonstrate their prowess through action.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes kriya (action) over vikalpa (vacillation). Kṛṣṇa's response to Jarāsandha's taunt exemplifies the yogi's silent, focused action.

teachings.teaching: The divine play (līlā) of Kṛṣṇa is beyond comprehension, yet described in human terms for our understanding.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary highlights that the apparent contradictions in Kṛṣṇa's actions (e.g., sparing Jarāsandha) are part of a larger cosmic plan, teaching surrender to the divine will.

chapter_summary: Krishna establishes his capital Dvaraka, receives gifts from the gods, and emerges unarmed to meet the approaching army.

story.title: Krishna's Glorious Capital and Unarmed Exit
story.arc: Krishna's establishment of Dvaraka and his peaceful approach to confrontation
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Dvaraka, before the battle with Jarasandha's army.
story.comic_potential: Krishna emerging unarmed with a garland, contrasting with the armed forces.

teachings.teaching: The divine gifts (Sudharma assembly hall, Parijata tree, horses, treasures) symbolize the grace of the gods upon the devotee who establishes dharma.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these gifts represent the siddhis (perfections) that arise naturally when one's practice is aligned with divine will; they are not to be sought but accepted as offerings.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's unarmed exit with a garland demonstrates that true strength lies in non-violence and trust in divine protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes ahimsa (non-violence) as a foundational practice; this act shows that a yogi can face adversaries without aggression, relying on inner power.

chapter_summary: Mucukunda, a king from the Ikshvaku dynasty, had been guarding the gods for a long time. After being granted a boon, he chose to sleep in a cave. The Yavana king, pursuing Krishna, entered the cave and kicked Mucukunda, who then burned him to ashes with his angry glance. Krishna revealed himself to Mucukunda, who recognized him as Narayana and offered prayers, realizing the futility of worldly attachments.

story.title: Mucukunda's Deliverance
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna killed Kamsa and before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: The Yavana chasing Krishna, who leads him into a cave where a sleeping giant wakes up and burns him with a glance, is visually striking and humorous.

teachings.teaching: The world is deluded by Maya and attached to sense objects, neglecting the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the state of ajnana (ignorance) where the practitioner is caught in vasanas (conditionings) and fails to turn inward to the Self.

teachings.teaching: Human life is rare and should be used to worship the Lord, not wasted in worldly pursuits.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the human body is the best vehicle for yoga; wasting it is like a blind man ignoring a gem.

teachings.teaching: Time, as the Lord, eventually destroys all attachments and pride.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching that kala (time) is a manifestation of the Lord, and the yogi must realize the impermanence of the body and world.

chapter_summary: King Mucukunda, after being awakened by Kṛṣṇa, recounts his past life as a king who conquered the world but ultimately realized the futility of material pursuits. He praises the association of saints and seeks refuge in Kṛṣṇa, rejecting all boons except unwavering devotion. Kṛṣṇa grants him devotion and assures him of liberation in his next life as a brāhmaṇa.

story.title: Mucukunda's Liberation
story.arc: Mucukunda, after a long sleep, meets Kṛṣṇa and renounces worldly desires, seeking only devotion. Kṛṣṇa blesses him with unwavering devotion and promises liberation in his next birth.
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Kālayavana, before the start of the Mahābhārata war.
story.comic_potential: High: Mucukunda's awakening and realization, his poetic lament, and Kṛṣṇa's compassionate response offer dramatic and philosophical depth.

teachings.teaching: Material achievements and sensual pleasures are temporary and lead to suffering; true happiness lies in devotion to God.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend the guṇas and realize the Self through disciplined practice, not through external acquisitions.

teachings.teaching: Association with saints (sat-saṅga) is the means to attain devotion and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes the importance of satsaṅga as a catalyst for inner transformation, aligning with the verse's teaching.

teachings.teaching: A devotee should not desire anything other than service to the Lord, even liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga ideal of niṣkāma karma and surrender to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: Unwavering devotion (ekānta-bhakti) cannot be shaken by any boon or temptation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's path of bhakti-yoga stresses single-pointed devotion as the highest sadhana.

teachings.teaching: Even for non-devotees, practices like prāṇāyāma are insufficient if vāsanās remain; devotion purifies the mind.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga includes prāṇāyāma but emphasizes that it must be coupled with devotion to uproot vāsanās.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the events leading to Krishna's marriage with Rukmini. After the encounter with Mucukunda, Krishna returns to Dvaraka, defeats Jarāsandha's army, and later marries Rukmini, the princess of Vidarbha, by abducting her in the Rākṣasa style after defeating her brother Rukmi and other suitors like Śiśupāla.

story.title: The Marriage of Krishna and Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the Mucukunda episode, before the burning of Khandava forest.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic escape from Jarāsandha, secret message, and daring abduction.

teachings.teaching: Contentment is the greatest wealth; even a poor contented man sleeps happily, while an discontented king suffers.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, contentment (santoṣa) is a key niyama that stabilizes the mind for meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is easily pleased by sincere devotion, not by mere external rituals.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga internalizes worship; the Lord responds to inner surrender, not outer show.

teachings.teaching: One should seek the shelter of the Lord's lotus feet to destroy ignorance, as even great beings like Śiva desire that.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, the dust of the guru's feet symbolizes the transmission of grace that removes tamas.

chapter_summary: Krishna, having received Rukmini's message, decides to abduct her before her forced marriage to Shishupala. He travels to Kundina with a brahmana messenger. Meanwhile, Rukmini's father prepares for the wedding, and many enemy kings assemble to prevent Krishna. Rukmini anxiously awaits Krishna, and upon hearing of his arrival, she goes to the temple of Goddess Ambika to pray. After the prayer, she leaves the temple, and Krishna abducts her.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmini
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Rukmini sends a message to Krishna and before the wedding ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: secret message, anxious bride, enemy kings assembling, last-minute abduction from temple.

teachings.teaching: Krishna reciprocates the devotee's love and takes action to fulfill their desire.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sadhana' where the practitioner's intense longing (Rukmini's devotion) invokes divine response (Krishna's intervention).

teachings.teaching: One should surrender to the divine will and not be anxious about outcomes.
teachings.lens_note: Rukmini's anxiety and eventual trust in Krishna mirrors the Kriya-Yoga teaching of 'Ishvara pranidhana' – surrender to the Lord.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects those who take refuge in Him.
teachings.lens_note: Krishna's abduction of Rukmini from the temple symbolizes the divine's protective intervention when the devotee is in distress, akin to the Kriya-Yoga concept of 'grace'.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī, adorned with jewels, walks gracefully to the temple, captivating the kings who become infatuated. She boards Krishna's chariot, and Krishna abducts her in the presence of the assembled kings, including Jarāsandha, who feel humiliated.

story.title: The Abduction of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Krishna's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: During the svayamvara ceremony
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic entrance, infatuated kings, daring abduction

teachings.teaching: Divine beauty can overwhelm worldly attachments.
The Lord's actions transcend social norms for divine purposes.

chapter_summary: After Rukmiṇī's abduction, Śiśupāla and other kings attack Kṛṣṇa but are defeated. Rukmī pursues Kṛṣṇa, is humiliated in battle, and spared at Rukmiṇī's plea. Balarāma rebukes Kṛṣṇa for disfiguring Rukmī and teaches Rukmiṇī about the nature of the self.

story.title: The Defeat of Śiśupāla and Rukmī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's marriage to Rukmiṇī
story.timeline_note: Immediately after Kṛṣṇa abducts Rukmiṇī from her svayaṃvara.
story.comic_potential: Rukmī's boastful challenge and subsequent humiliation, with Kṛṣṇa cutting his weapons and shaving his head, has darkly comic elements.

teachings.teaching: The self (ātman) is one in all beings; distinctions of friend, foe, and neutral arise from ignorance of the body.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this points to the realization of the unified consciousness (cit) beyond the ego, which is the goal of yoga.

teachings.teaching: The body is temporary and subject to change; the self is eternal and unaffected.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga teaching of disidentification from the body and senses to realize the immortal self.

teachings.teaching: Sorrow born of ignorance can be removed by knowledge of the truth (tattva-jñāna).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga is the practical means to attain this knowledge through meditation and self-inquiry.

chapter_summary: Rukmiṇī is abducted by Kṛṣṇa and brought to Dvārakā, where they are married with great celebration. Rukmin, enraged, builds a new city and vows not to return to Kuṇḍina until he kills Kṛṣṇa. The kings and princesses are astonished by the abduction.

story.title: The Abduction and Marriage of Rukmiṇī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa abducts Rukmiṇī from her svayamvara and marries her in Dvārakā, while Rukmin vows revenge.
story.timeline_note: After the svayamvara of Rukmiṇī, Kṛṣṇa takes her to Dvārakā and marries her.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic abduction, the enraged brother building a new city, and the grand wedding celebrations offer vivid scenes.

teachings.teaching: One should abandon mental distress and fix the mind on intelligence (buddhi).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this indicates the practice of pratyahara (withdrawal) and dharana (concentration) to overcome emotional turmoil.

teachings.teaching: The Lord marries according to Vedic rites, showing the importance of dharma in relationships.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that even divine actions follow scriptural injunctions, teaching the value of discipline in yoga.

teachings.teaching: Celebration and devotion arise naturally when the Lord is present.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga principle that inner joy (ananda) manifests when the self is united with the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of Pradyumna, the son of Krishna and Rukmini, who is an incarnation of Kamadeva. As a newborn, he is stolen by the demon Shambara and thrown into the ocean, where a fish swallows him. The fish is caught and brought to Shambara's kitchen, where Mayavati (Rati in disguise) discovers the baby. Narada reveals the truth to her, and she raises Pradyumna. When he grows up, Rati reveals his identity and gives him the Mahamaya vidya. Pradyumna challenges Shambara, defeats him using the vidya, and beheads him. He then returns to Dvaraka with Rati, where he is joyfully reunited with his family.

story.title: The Return of Pradyumna
story.arc: Pradyumna's abduction, growth, and victorious return to Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Rukmini and before the later pastimes in Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic abduction, fish swallowing baby, demon's kitchen, identity reveal, battle with magical weapons, and joyful reunion.

teachings.teaching: The divine plan unfolds even through apparent tragedies; the demon's attempt to kill Pradyumna leads to his growth and eventual victory.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates how obstacles (kleshas) are transformed into opportunities for spiritual growth when one surrenders to the divine will.

teachings.teaching: The power of divine knowledge (Mahamaya vidya) overcomes all illusions (mayas).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Kriya-Yoga practice of pranayama and meditation destroys the maya of the mind, just as the vidya given by Mayavati destroys Shambara's illusions.

teachings.teaching: Recognition of the divine through inner realization, not external appearance.
teachings.lens_note: Rukmini's intuitive recognition of Pradyumna despite his changed form mirrors the Kriya-Yoga practitioner's recognition of the inner Self beyond the body.

chapter_summary: This chapter narrates the story of the Syamantaka jewel. Satrajit, a devotee of the Sun god, receives the jewel from Surya. His brother Prasena is killed by a lion while wearing it, and the lion is killed by Jambavan. Krishna is falsely accused of taking the jewel. To clear his name, Krishna tracks the jewel to Jambavan's cave, fights him for 28 days, and ultimately Jambavan recognizes Krishna as the Supreme Lord. Jambavan offers his daughter Jambavati and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna returns the jewel to Satrajit, who, repentant, gives his daughter Satyabhama and the jewel to Krishna. Krishna accepts Satyabhama but returns the jewel to Satrajit.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel
story.arc: Krishna clears his name of false accusation, marries Satyabhama and Jambavati, and demonstrates his divine supremacy.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Krishna's residence in Dvaraka, after his marriage to Rukmini.
story.comic_potential: The mistaken identity of Satrajit as Surya, the fight with Jambavan, and the dramatic return from the cave.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects the reputation of his devotees and clears false accusations.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'sattva-shuddhi' (purification of being) where the divine intervenes to restore truth and harmony.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth (the jewel) can cause conflict and suffering, but when offered to the Lord, it becomes a source of grace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes 'vairagya' (detachment) and 'bhakti' (devotion) as means to transform material objects into spiritual instruments.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate reality, the source of all power, and the inner self of all beings.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's teaching of 'atma-jnana' (self-knowledge) where one recognizes the divine presence within.

teachings.teaching: Repentance and offering one's all to the Lord leads to forgiveness and peace.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga stresses 'prayashchitta' (atonement) through selfless action and surrender.

chapter_summary: Krishna and Balarama, after hearing of the Pandavas' suffering, visit Hastinapura to console them. Meanwhile, Satrajit is murdered by Shatadhanva for the Syamantaka jewel. Shatadhanva flees, pursued by Krishna and Balarama. Krishna kills Shatadhanva but the jewel is not found. Balarama suspects Krishna and goes to Mithila, where he learns gadā from Janaka. Krishna returns to Dvārakā, performs funeral rites for Satrajit, and later retrieves the jewel from Akrūra, clearing his name.

story.title: The Syamantaka Jewel and the Clearing of Krishna's Name
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: After the Pandavas' exile, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: High drama with murder, chase, and a jewel theft; Balarama's suspicion and Krishna's clever resolution.

teachings.teaching: Krishna, though omniscient, acts within human conventions to teach dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This illustrates the principle of 'lila' — divine play that follows human norms to guide practitioners in righteous action.

teachings.teaching: The wise do not oppose the will of the Lord, as seen in Akrūra's refusal to help Shatadhanva.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Surrender to the divine will (ishvara-pranidhana) is a key practice; resisting leads to downfall.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's pastimes demonstrate that even the Lord faces false accusations, but truth prevails through righteous conduct.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: This teaches the importance of patience and faith in the face of adversity; one's actions should be aligned with dharma, leaving results to the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Syamantaka jewel symbolizes material wealth that causes conflict; true wealth is devotion to Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Attachment to objects (asakti) leads to suffering; the practice of vairagya (detachment) is essential for inner peace.

chapter_summary: Krishna visits the Pandavas in Indraprastha, marries Kalindi (daughter of Surya) and later Satya (daughter of Nagnajit) after subduing seven bulls, and also marries Mitravinda. The chapter recounts Arjuna's forest hunt, the burning of Khandava forest, and the construction of Indraprastha.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages and the Pandavas
story.arc: Krishna's earthly pastimes
story.timeline_note: After the burning of Khandava forest and construction of Indraprastha, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna subduing seven bulls with ease, and Arjuna's hunting spree.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's presence removes all sins (aṅga-saṅga-hatainasaḥ).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this signifies that contact with the divine (through meditation or association) purifies the karmic traces.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the inner witness who removes the sufferings of those who remember Him (smaratāṃ śaśvat kleśān haṃsi hṛdi sthitaḥ).
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga practice of constant remembrance (smaraṇa) as a means to transcend suffering.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-satisfied (ātmānandena pūrṇasya) and needs nothing.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the yogi, through Kriya, attains inner fullness and acts without personal desire.

chapter_summary: Krishna marries many wives, including Satya (daughter of King Nagnajit of Kosala), Bhadra (daughter of Shrutakirti, his father's sister), Lakshmana (daughter of the Madra king, taken by force in a svayamvara), and thousands of other women rescued after killing the demon Bhauma. The chapter describes the wedding processions, the defeat of rival kings by Arjuna, and Krishna's return to Dvaraka.

story.title: Krishna's Marriages to Satya, Bhadra, Lakshmana, and Others
story.arc: Krishna's multiple marriages and the defeat of Bhauma
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Bhauma (Narakasura), Krishna marries the captive women and then marries Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana.
story.comic_potential: The svayamvara of Lakshmana where Krishna abducts her like Garuda taking nectar, and the defeated kings crying on the road.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's marriages demonstrate his divine pastimes and his role as the supreme enjoyer, yet he remains unattached.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'kriya' (action) performed without attachment, as Krishna engages in worldly activities while remaining transcendent.

teachings.teaching: The defeat of rival kings by Arjuna shows the protection of dharma by the Lord's devotee.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the devotee's actions, when aligned with the divine will, become instruments of cosmic order.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna, accompanied by his wife Satyabhama, travels to the city of Pragjyotisha to defeat the demon Narakasura. After a fierce battle, Krishna kills Naraka and his sons, retrieves the earrings of Aditi, and rescues 16,000 captive princesses. He then returns the earrings to Indra, uproots the Parijata tree for Satyabhama, and marries the princesses in multiple forms.

story.title: The Slaying of Narakasura and the Rescue of the Princesses
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Krishna's marriage to Satyabhama and before the Parijata episode.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic battle scenes, Krishna's multiple forms marrying princesses, and the comedic uprooting of the Parijata tree.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys demons, demonstrating his omnipotence and compassion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the inner battle against negative tendencies (demons) and the cultivation of divine qualities through disciplined practice.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord (prapatti) brings liberation from fear and sin.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that surrender is the culmination of Kriya-Yoga, where the individual ego dissolves into the divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are beyond material comprehension; even Brahma cannot fully understand them.
teachings.lens_note: This highlights the limitation of intellectual knowledge and the need for direct experience through yoga.

chapter_summary: Krishna teases Rukmini about her choice of him as husband, pretending to be unworthy and suggesting she marry a king instead. Rukmini responds with a passionate defense of her devotion, explaining that she chose him as the Supreme Lord despite his apparent poverty. Krishna then reveals his teasing was a test of her love and affirms her pure devotion.

story.title: Krishna's Teasing of Rukmini
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, early in their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's mock self-deprecation and Rukmini's earnest rebuttal create a playful yet profound dialogue.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is self-sufficient and not motivated by personal desire; he acts for the upliftment of devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of 'nishkama karma' – action without personal desire, performed for the welfare of others.

teachings.teaching: True devotion is unconditional and not based on material considerations.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the devotee must transcend the three gunas and offer service without expectation, as Rukmini exemplifies.

teachings.teaching: The Lord tests his devotees to strengthen their love and faith.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga concept of 'pariksha' – divine testing that purifies the heart and deepens surrender.

teachings.teaching: Material wealth and power are insignificant compared to devotion to the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that the goal of yoga is to realize the self as distinct from the body and material possessions, leading to detachment.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the ultimate refuge and fulfiller of all desires for his devotees.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this is the state of 'prapatti' – complete surrender, where the Lord takes responsibility for the devotee's spiritual and material well-being.

chapter_summary: Rukmini expresses her deep love and devotion to Krishna, recounting how she chose him over other kings and how he rescued her. Krishna responds with playful banter, and the chapter concludes with their loving exchange and Krishna's domestic life with his queens.

story.title: Rukmini's Loving Lament and Krishna's Playful Response
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes with his queens
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to Rukmini, during their domestic life.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's teasing and Rukmini's affectionate complaints create a light-hearted, romantic comedy.

teachings.teaching: The devotee's loving complaints to the Lord are a form of intimate devotion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the stage of 'sakha-bhava' where the devotee relates to the divine as a friend or lover, expressing raw emotions as a means of union.

teachings.teaching: The Lord, though self-satisfied, engages in human-like pastimes to teach the path of devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Krishna's 'viḍambayan' (imitating human behavior) is a teaching tool, showing that divine love can be expressed through ordinary human interactions.

chapter_summary: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (Rukmi's daughter), and narrates the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, which ends with Rukmi's death at Balarama's hands.

story.title: Chapter 61
story.arc: This chapter lists the sons of Krishna by his chief queens, describes the marriage of Aniruddha to Rocana (Rukmi's daughter), and narrates the dice game between Balarama and Rukmi, which ends with Rukmi's death at Balarama's hands.

chapter_summary: King Parikshit asks Shuka about the marriage of Aniruddha to Usha, daughter of Bana, and the ensuing battle between Krishna and Shiva. Shuka narrates Bana's boon from Shiva, his arrogance, Usha's dream of Aniruddha, Citralekha's abduction of Aniruddha, and Bana's capture of Aniruddha.

story.title: The Marriage of Aniruddha and Usha
story.arc: Usha dreams of Aniruddha, Citralekha abducts him, Bana captures him, leading to a battle between Krishna and Shiva.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's incarnation, during the time of Bana's rule.
story.comic_potential: High: dream romance, secret abduction, arrogant demon, impending divine battle.

teachings.teaching: Arrogance leads to downfall; Bana's pride in his thousand arms is humbled by Shiva's prophecy.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the need to surrender ego (ahamkara) to the divine will, as Bana's pride is a barrier to spiritual progress.

teachings.teaching: Divine love transcends boundaries; Usha's love for Aniruddha is predestined and fulfilled through divine intervention.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework sees this as the soul's (jiva) longing for union with the Supreme (Ishvara), facilitated by grace (Citralekha as guru).

teachings.teaching: The power of yoga (Citralekha's yogic ability) to bring about divine union.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this represents the advanced yogic siddhi of transporting consciousness, symbolizing the guru's ability to connect the disciple with the divine.

chapter_summary: Lord Krishna and Balarama lead the Vrishnis to rescue Aniruddha from Bana's fortress. A fierce battle ensues between Krishna and Shiva, with various duels. Krishna defeats Shiva's forces, cuts off Bana's arms, and spares him due to Brahma's boon and Shiva's request. Bana surrenders and returns Aniruddha.

story.title: The Rescue of Aniruddha
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Aniruddha's captivity by Bana; four months pass before rescue.
story.comic_potential: High: cosmic battle with gods, demons, and fevers; Bana's thousand arms cut off; Kotara's naked intervention.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the supreme controller, beyond all material categories.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Recognizing Krishna as the inner self (atman) is the goal of yoga; the external battle symbolizes internal struggle with ego.

teachings.teaching: Surrender to the Lord is the ultimate refuge; even demons can be saved.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: Surrender (prapatti) is the essence of bhakti; Bana's survival despite sin illustrates grace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's avatars relieve the earth's burden and protect dharma.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The descent of the divine (avatara) is a model for the yogi's descent into the body to uplift consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Material existence is a cycle of suffering; one should seek the Lord's feet.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga: The 'vijnarnava' (ocean of sin) is the cycle of karma; meditation on Krishna's feet is the raft.

chapter_summary: Krishna returns to his capital with his wives, welcomed by citizens and brahmanas. The chapter concludes with a verse stating that one who recites this victory of Krishna over Shiva in the morning will never face defeat.

story.title: Krishna's Return to Dvaraka
story.arc: Krishna's victory over Shiva and return to his capital
story.timeline_note: After the battle with Shiva, Krishna returns to Dvaraka.
story.comic_potential: A grand procession with decorated streets, conches, and drums, and the king being welcomed by citizens.

teachings.teaching: Reciting Krishna's victory over Shiva in the morning ensures one never faces defeat.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the power of daily remembrance (smriti) of divine victories to strengthen one's inner resolve and overcome obstacles.

chapter_summary: King Nṛga, a generous king of the Ikṣvāku dynasty, once gave a cow to a brāhmaṇa, but the cow had strayed into his herd and was mistakenly given. When the original owner and the recipient both claimed the cow, Nṛga offered compensation, but both refused. He died and was turned into a lizard in a well. After a long time, Kṛṣṇa and the Yādava boys found him, and Kṛṣṇa lifted him out, restoring his divine form. Nṛga narrated his story, praised Kṛṣṇa, and ascended to heaven. Kṛṣṇa then taught the Yādavas about the grave sin of misappropriating brāhmaṇa property.

story.title: The Deliverance of King Nṛga
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes in Dvārakā
story.timeline_note: Occurs during Kṛṣṇa's residence in Dvārakā, after the Yādava boys go to play in a garden.
story.comic_potential: High: A king turned into a lizard, rescued by Kṛṣṇa, then a moral lesson about brāhmaṇa property.

teachings.teaching: Misappropriating brāhmaṇa property, even unknowingly, leads to severe punishment in hell.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the importance of purity in action (kriya) and the karmic consequences of even unintentional transgressions against those who are dedicated to spiritual practice.

teachings.teaching: One should never harm a brāhmaṇa, even if he is at fault; instead, bow to him always.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework highlights humility and non-retaliation as essential for spiritual progress, aligning with the principle of ahimsa and respect for all beings, especially those on the path.

teachings.teaching: The sin of stealing brāhmaṇa property is worse than poison and destroys the family for generations.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching underscores the gravity of actions that disrupt the spiritual ecosystem; in Kriya-Yoga, such acts create deep karmic impressions that hinder liberation.

chapter_summary: Balarama visits Vrindavan, meets the gopas and gopis, consoles them with messages from Krishna, enjoys the Yamuna forest with women, drinks varuni, becomes intoxicated, and in a fit of anger drags the Yamuna river with his plow. The river goddess submits, and Balarama releases her, then bathes and enjoys.

story.title: Balarama's Visit to Vrindavan and the Yamuna Incident
story.arc: Balarama's pastimes in Vraja
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Mathura, Balarama visits Vrindavan during the months of Madhu and Madhava (spring).
story.comic_potential: Balarama's drunken antics, dragging the river, and the river goddess's plea create a humorous and dramatic scene.

teachings.teaching: The gopis' intense love for Krishna is expressed through their lamentation and questioning of his absence.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the soul's longing for union with the Divine, where the gopis represent the individual soul (jiva) yearning for the Supreme Self (Krishna).

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger and subsequent mercy towards Yamuna shows the Lord's power over nature and his compassion when approached with humility.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the control of prana (Balarama) over the nadis (Yamuna), and the need for surrender to the divine will.

chapter_summary: King Pauṇḍraka, deluded by his playmates into believing he is Vāsudeva (Kṛṣṇa), sends a messenger to Kṛṣṇa demanding that Kṛṣṇa give up his insignia. Kṛṣṇa laughs, warns him, and then goes to fight. Kṛṣṇa kills Pauṇḍraka and his ally the King of Kāśī. The King of Kāśī's son Sudakṣiṇa performs a ritual to create a fiery demon to avenge his father. The demon attacks Dvārakā, but Kṛṣṇa sends his Sudarśana cakra, which destroys the demon and burns Vārāṇasī. The cakra then returns to Kṛṣṇa.

story.title: The Killing of Pauṇḍraka and the Burning of Vārāṇasī
story.arc: Kṛṣṇa's pastimes as the supreme lord, demonstrating his power and protecting his devotees.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after Kṛṣṇa's return to Dvārakā from various pastimes.
story.comic_potential: Pauṇḍraka's delusion and imitation of Kṛṣṇa's appearance, Kṛṣṇa's laughter, the fiery demon's attack, and the cakra's destruction.

teachings.teaching: Delusion and false pride lead to destruction. Pauṇḍraka's claim to be Vāsudeva results in his death.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the danger of identifying with the ego (ahamkara) rather than the true Self. Pauṇḍraka's imitation of Kṛṣṇa's external form without inner realization is a caution against superficial spiritual practices.

teachings.teaching: The Lord protects his devotees and destroys the wicked. Kṛṣṇa sends his cakra to save Dvārakā.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that the divine power (cakra) is the active aspect of consciousness that dissolves obstacles. The devotee's surrender invokes this protection.

teachings.teaching: Rituals performed with malicious intent (abhicāra) backfire on the performer. The demon created by Sudakṣiṇa ultimately destroys him.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the law of karma: actions motivated by hatred return to the doer. In Kriya-Yoga, purification of intention is essential before any practice.

chapter_summary: King Parīkṣit asks Śuka about Balarāma's extraordinary deeds. Śuka narrates the story of Dvivida, a monkey friend of Narakāsura, who wreaks havoc across the land to avenge his friend. He disrupts sacrifices, imprisons people, and finally confronts Balarāma on Raivataka mountain. After a fierce battle, Balarāma kills Dvivida with his fist, to the acclaim of celestial beings.

story.title: The Slaying of Dvivida
story.arc: Balarāma's pastimes
story.timeline_note: After Narakāsura's death, Dvivida seeks revenge.
story.comic_potential: Dvivida's antics, including imitating Balarāma's wives and breaking the wine pot, have comedic elements.

teachings.teaching: The divine protects the righteous and destroys the wicked.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of karma: actions have consequences, and divine intervention restores cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: Even a powerful demon cannot withstand the Lord's might.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes that the ego (Dvivida) is ultimately subdued by the higher Self (Balarāma) through disciplined practice.

chapter_summary: Samba, son of Krishna and Jambavati, abducts Lakshmana, daughter of Duryodhana, from her svayamvara. The Kauravas capture him. Balarama goes to Hastinapura as a peace envoy but is insulted. Enraged, he uproots the city with his plow and threatens to throw it into the Ganges. The Kauravas surrender, release Samba and Lakshmana, and offer tribute. Balarama forgives them.

story.title: The Abduction of Lakshmana and Balarama's Wrath
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Pandavas' exile but before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's rage and the city being uprooted like a toy is visually spectacular.

teachings.teaching: Balarama's anger is for the sake of teaching, not out of hatred or envy.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates that divine anger is a tool for correction, not personal emotion. The sage uses controlled force to restore dharma.

teachings.teaching: The Kauravas' pride leads to their downfall; humility and surrender bring protection.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that ego (ahamkara) is the root of bondage. Surrender (prapatti) to the divine dissolves karma and grants peace.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is the sole support and controller of all worlds; even kings are mere instruments.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga view that the individual self (jiva) is a fragment of the Supreme. Realizing this unity is the goal of practice.

chapter_summary: Balarama returns to Dvaraka after his pilgrimage, recounts his adventures to the Yadava assembly, and the city still bears marks of his prowess.

story.title: Balarama's Return and Report
story.arc: Balarama's pilgrimage and return
story.timeline_note: After the events of the pilgrimage described in previous chapters.
story.comic_potential: Balarama's dramatic recounting and the visible marks of his strength on the city.

teachings.teaching: The importance of sharing one's experiences with the community.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this reflects the principle of sanga (association) and the sharing of spiritual experiences to inspire others.

chapter_summary: Narada, curious about Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple households after marrying 16,100 wives, visits Dvaraka. He witnesses Krishna in various activities across different homes, demonstrating his yoga-maya. Narada praises Krishna and departs, understanding the divine play.

story.title: Narada's Visit to Dvaraka: The Yoga-Maya of Krishna
story.arc: Narada's curiosity leads him to witness Krishna's simultaneous presence in all his households, demonstrating his divine yoga-maya.
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's marriage to 16,100 wives and the defeat of Narakasura.
story.comic_potential: Narada's bewildered expressions as he sees Krishna in different roles simultaneously, like a cosmic multitasker.

teachings.teaching: Krishna's simultaneous presence in multiple forms demonstrates his yoga-maya, the divine power to be everywhere at once.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this illustrates the principle of 'eka-vapuḥ aneka-bhāva' — one body, many states, achievable through yogic integration of all life activities.

teachings.teaching: Krishna engages in all aspects of human life (dharma, artha, kama) without attachment, setting an example for householders.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga does not reject worldly life but integrates it as a field for spiritual practice, mirroring Krishna's balanced engagement.

teachings.teaching: Narada's humility and devotion are rewarded with deeper insight into Krishna's divine nature.
teachings.lens_note: This reflects the Kriya-Yoga path of surrender and service, leading to direct realization of the divine.

chapter_summary: Krishna's daily routine in Dvaraka, including his morning activities, worship, and courtly life. He receives a messenger from imprisoned kings seeking liberation from Jarāsandha, and Nārada arrives to request Krishna's approval for Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya sacrifice.

story.title: Krishna's Daily Routine and the Plea of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka; preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After Krishna's return to Dvaraka, before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: Krishna's elaborate morning routine contrasts with the sudden arrival of a distressed messenger and the sage Nārada.

teachings.teaching: One should rise early in the brāhma-muhūrta and meditate on the Supreme Self.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this aligns with the practice of early morning meditation (dhyāna) to stabilize the mind in the Self.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme Lord is self-luminous, beyond material qualities, and the cause of creation and destruction.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes realizing the Self as distinct from the body-mind complex, which is the goal of Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Charity to brāhmaṇas and worship of deities, sages, and ancestors are part of daily duty.
teachings.lens_note: Kriya-Yoga includes karma-yoga as a means to purify the mind; such actions are performed without attachment.

teachings.teaching: The material happiness of kings is like a dream, dependent on others, and full of fear.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that sense pleasures are transient; true happiness is found in the Self through Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Hearing, chanting, and meditating on the Lord purify even the lowest.
teachings.lens_note: These are key practices in Kriya-Yoga for inner purification and God-realization.

chapter_summary: Uddhava advises Krishna to kill Jarāsandha to enable the Rājasūya sacrifice, and Krishna agrees. The Yadava army travels to Indraprastha, where Yudhiṣṭhira and the Pāṇḍavas welcome them. Krishna stays for several months, enjoying pastimes with Arjuna.

story.title: Uddhava's Advice and Krishna's Journey to Indraprastha
story.arc: Preparation for Rājasūya
story.timeline_note: After Nārada's visit, Uddhava speaks; Krishna then travels to Indraprastha and stays for several months.
story.comic_potential: The grand procession of the Yadava army and the emotional reunion of Krishna with the Pāṇḍavas.

teachings.teaching: Killing Jarāsandha is necessary for the Rājasūya sacrifice and for the welfare of many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the need to overcome internal obstacles (Jarāsandha as ego) to achieve higher spiritual goals (Rājasūya as self-realization).

teachings.teaching: One should use appropriate means (Bhīma in brahmin disguise) to defeat a powerful enemy.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes strategic action (kriya) aligned with dharma, using wisdom to overcome brute force.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's pastimes are sung by devotees for liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, singing and hearing Krishna's glories purifies the mind and leads to inner stillness.

teachings.teaching: The Lord's arrival brings joy and liberation to all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views Krishna's presence as the state of pure consciousness that dissolves all sorrow.

chapter_summary: Yudhiṣṭhira expresses his desire to perform the Rājasūya sacrifice. Kṛṣṇa advises him to first conquer all kings. Yudhiṣṭhira sends his brothers to subdue the directions. They bring tribute, but Jarāsandha remains unsubdued. Kṛṣṇa, Bhīma, and Arjuna disguise as brāhmaṇas, go to Girivraja, and challenge Jarāsandha to a duel. After a prolonged fight, Kṛṣṇa signals Bhīma to split Jarāsandha's body, killing him. Kṛṣṇa then releases the imprisoned kings and installs Jarāsandha's son Sahadeva as king of Magadha.

story.title: The Killing of Jarāsandha
story.arc: Preparation for the Rājasūya sacrifice
story.timeline_note: After the Pāṇḍavas' exile and before the Rājasūya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: High: dramatic duel, disguise, and a decisive split-body kill.

teachings.teaching: One should perform righteous acts like the Rājasūya sacrifice to gain fame and please the Lord.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this represents the culmination of selfless action (karma-yoga) where the devotee offers all results to the Divine.

teachings.teaching: The Lord is impartial and bestows grace according to one's devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the Lord's grace is like a wish-fulfilling tree: it responds proportionally to the devotee's surrender and effort.

teachings.teaching: Even an unstable body can be used to attain eternal glory.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga principle of using the impermanent body as a tool for spiritual evolution and lasting merit.

teachings.teaching: Examples of great beings who attained the eternal through the impermanent.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma cites these as historical proofs that sincere practice of dharma, even in adversity, leads to liberation.

chapter_summary: Krishna releases the kings imprisoned by Jarasandha, who praise him and renounce worldly attachments. Krishna instructs them on devotion and righteous rule, then honors them. He has Bhima kill Jarasandha, and returns to Indraprastha with the Pandavas, where Yudhishthira rejoices.

story.title: Liberation of the Imprisoned Kings
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Mathura and Dvaraka
story.timeline_note: After the killing of Jarasandha, before the Rajasuya sacrifice.
story.comic_potential: The dramatic release of the kings, their awe at Krishna's appearance, and the subsequent battle with Jarasandha.

teachings.teaching: Wealth and power lead to pride and downfall; one should seek refuge in Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this echoes the need to transcend ego (ahamkara) through surrender to the divine will, aligning individual action with cosmic order.

teachings.teaching: The material world is like a mirage; only the Supreme is real.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's framework emphasizes discerning the eternal (sat) from the transient (asat), a core practice of viveka in Kriya-Yoga.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Krishna leads to liberation from samsara.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with the Kriya-Yoga path of bhakti as a means to still the mind and realize the Self.

teachings.teaching: One should perform duties without attachment, with mind fixed on Krishna.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary on karma-yoga: action as worship, performed with equanimity.

chapter_summary: Yudhishthira expresses gratitude to Krishna for the defeat of Jarasandha and arranges the Rajasuya sacrifice. During the ceremony, Sahadeva proposes that Krishna be honored first, which enrages Shishupala. Shishupala insults Krishna and is beheaded by Krishna's discus. His soul merges into Krishna. The sacrifice concludes successfully.

story.title: The Rajasuya Sacrifice and the Death of Shishupala
story.arc: Krishna's pastimes in Dvaraka and the Pandavas' rise
story.timeline_note: After the defeat of Jarasandha, Yudhishthira performs the Rajasuya sacrifice. Shishupala's death occurs during the ceremony.
story.comic_potential: High drama: Shishupala's outburst, Krishna's silent calm, and the sudden beheading. The assembly's reactions and the divine light merging into Krishna provide visual spectacle.

teachings.teaching: Krishna is the supreme, non-dual reality, the source of all beings and sacrifices.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this affirms that the ultimate goal of all practices is to realize the oneness of the individual self with the Supreme Self, Krishna.

teachings.teaching: Honoring Krishna is equivalent to honoring all beings and the self.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga emphasizes that service to the divine is the highest form of self-realization, as the divine pervades all.

teachings.teaching: One who hears blasphemy of the Lord and does not leave is deprived of piety.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the importance of association with the divine and avoiding negative influences, a key principle in Kriya-Yoga for maintaining spiritual purity.

teachings.teaching: Constant enmity and meditation on the Lord, even in hatred, can lead to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that intense focus on the divine, regardless of the emotion, can purify the mind and lead to union, as seen in Shishupala's case.

chapter_summary: After the Rajasuya sacrifice, all celestial and human guests depart, praising Krishna and the sacrifice. Only the sinful Duryodhana, envious of the Pandavas' prosperity, remains resentful. The chapter concludes with a verse glorifying the recitation of Krishna's deeds, including the killing of Shishupala and the liberation of the king.

story.title: Duryodhana's Envy and the Glorification of Krishna
story.arc: After the Rajasuya sacrifice, Duryodhana's jealousy foreshadows the Kurukshetra war.
story.timeline_note: Occurs after the Rajasuya sacrifice of Yudhishthira, before the Kurukshetra war.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana's brooding envy contrasted with the joyful departure of others; a dramatic moment of silent resentment.

teachings.teaching: Reciting Krishna's deeds (like killing Shishupala) liberates from all sins.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the purifying power of meditative remembrance (smarana) of divine acts, which aligns with the practice of constant mindfulness of the Divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter describes the Rājasūya sacrifice of King Yudhiṣṭhira, the grand festivities including the avabhṛtha bath, the distribution of gifts, and the departure of guests. It also narrates Duryodhana's envy upon seeing the opulence, his humiliation due to Māyā's illusion in the assembly hall, and his angry departure, which foreshadows the Kurukṣetra war.

story.title: Yudhiṣṭhira's Rājasūya and Duryodhana's Humiliation
story.arc: The chapter details the successful completion of the Rājasūya sacrifice, the joyous celebrations, and the subsequent envy and humiliation of Duryodhana, which sows the seeds for the great war.
story.timeline_note: This event occurs after the Pāṇḍavas have established their kingdom and before the Kurukṣetra war.
